seasonalPresence: March to September. Breeding birds return from West Africa. Best viewing April to August.
bestTimeOfDay: Morning. Ospreys are most active fishing in the first few hours after dawn.
whereToSee: RSPB Haweswater is the flagship site. The nest is visible from the hide. Foulshaw Moss has a viewing platform. Bassenthwaite Lake has a nest with live webcam.
ukPopulation: Around 300 breeding pairs in the UK. Reintroduced to England in the 1990s.
lakesPopulation: RSPB Haweswater holds one of the most watched osprey nests in England. Foulshaw Moss and Bassenthwaite also have breeding pairs.
diet: Fish. Almost exclusively. Dives feet-first to catch prey.
description: The osprey is the star of Lake District wildlife. Reintroduced to England after extinction, they now breed at several Lakeland sites. RSPB Haweswater is the place to go. Get there before 10am if you want a decent spot at the hide. The nest is visible from the road. Take binoculars.
identification: Large raptor. White head and underparts, dark brown back. Distinctive M-shaped wing profile in flight. Yellow eyes.
tips: April to August is the breeding season. The hide at Haweswater can get busy at weekends. Midweek is quieter. Check the RSPB website for nest activity updates.

faq.question: When is the best time to see Ospreys in the Lake District?
faq.answer: April to August. Birds return from West Africa in March and are most visible when feeding chicks. RSPB Haweswater and Foulshaw Moss are the main sites.

faq.question: Where can I see Ospreys in the Lake District?
faq.answer: RSPB Haweswater, Foulshaw Moss near Grange-over-Sands, and Bassenthwaite Lake. Haweswater has a hide with good views of the nest.

seasonalPresence: Year-round. Reintroduced and now established across the Lake District.
bestTimeOfDay: Midday. Often seen soaring on thermals.
whereToSee: Grizedale Forest, the eastern fells, and the valleys around Windermere. Soaring over open hillside is the typical view.
ukPopulation: Over 4,000 breeding pairs. One of the UK's conservation success stories.
lakesPopulation: Established across the Lake District. Grizedale and the eastern valleys are reliable. Often seen from the road.
description: Red kites are everywhere in the Lakes now. Reintroduced in the 1990s, they've spread across Cumbria. You'll see them soaring, that distinctive forked tail, from most of the main valleys. Grizedale is a good bet.
identification: Large raptor with deeply forked tail. Reddish-brown body, pale head. Soars with wings held in a shallow V.
tips: Look up on clear days. They use thermals. Roadside laybys on the A591 and around Grizedale often give good views.

seasonalPresence: Year-round. Breeding on crags from March. Most visible April to July.
bestTimeOfDay: Morning and evening. Hunting flights are most likely when thermals build.
whereToSee: Crags throughout the fells. Honister Pass, Langdale Pikes, and the Borrowdale valley have reliable nesting sites. RSPB Haweswater sometimes has a pair.
ukPopulation: Around 1,500 breeding pairs in the UK.
lakesPopulation: Breed on crags across the Lake District. Honister and Langdale are among the most accessible. Respect nesting sites and keep distance.
diet: Birds. Pigeons, grouse, waders. Hunts in a characteristic stoop.
description: Peregrines nest on the crags. Honister, Langdale, Borrowdale. You'll hear them before you see them sometimes. That high-pitched kek-kek-kek. Keep your distance. Nesting birds are protected.
identification: Large falcon. Dark grey back, barred underparts. Distinctive black moustache. Fastest bird in the world when stooping.
tips: Scan crags with binoculars. Look for whitewash on ledges. Spring and early summer are best. Don't approach nests.

faq.question: Where can I see peregrine falcons in the Lake District?
faq.answer: Crags throughout the fells. Honister Pass, Langdale Pikes, and the Borrowdale valley have reliable nesting sites. RSPB Haweswater sometimes has a pair. Scan with binoculars.

faq.question: When is the best time to see peregrine falcons in the Lake District?
faq.answer: April to July. Breeding season. Most visible when feeding chicks. Morning and evening when thermals build. Don't approach nests. Nesting birds are protected.

faq.question: How do I identify a peregrine falcon in the Lake District?
faq.answer: Large falcon. Dark grey back, barred underparts. Distinctive black moustache. You'll hear the high-pitched kek-kek-kek before you see them. Fastest bird in the world when stooping.

seasonalPresence: Year-round. Most visible in spring when displaying.
whereToSee: Everywhere. The most common raptor in the Lakes. Any open hillside, valley, or woodland edge.
ukPopulation: Over 70,000 breeding pairs. Widespread across the UK.
lakesPopulation: Abundant. You'll struggle to do a day in the Lakes without seeing one. Often mistaken for eagles.
description: Buzzards are everywhere. The most common raptor in the Lakes. You'll see them from the car on most drives. Soaring, mewing. Don't confuse them with golden eagles. Buzzards are smaller and more numerous.
identification: Medium-sized raptor. Variable plumage from dark to pale. Broad wings, short tail. Often soars in circles.
tips: No tips needed. You'll see them. If you're not sure it's a buzzard, it probably is.

seasonalPresence: Breeding on the high fells April to July. Winter flocks on farmland and coastal marshes.
bestTimeOfDay: Dawn and dusk on the fells. Winter flocks visible throughout the day.
whereToSee: High fells in summer. Great Gable, the Helvellyn range, and the Coniston fells hold breeding birds. Winter flocks on farmland around the Lakes.
lakesPopulation: Breed on the highest Lake District fells. You need to walk to find them. Listen for the mournful call.
description: Golden plovers breed on the high moorland. You need to get up there. Great Gable, Helvellyn, the Coniston range. That mournful pee-oo call carries a long way. In winter they form flocks on farmland.
identification: Medium wader. Golden speckled back in summer. Black face and belly in breeding plumage. Winter birds are duller.
tips: Walk the high fells in May and June. They're ground-nesting so stick to paths. The call is distinctive once you know it.

seasonalPresence: March to September. Summer visitor. Arrives in March, leaves by October.
bestTimeOfDay: Morning. Sings from crags and boulder fields.
whereToSee: Upland crags and boulder fields. Langdale, Borrowdale, the Helvellyn range. Look for them around rocky terrain above the treeline.
ukPopulation: Around 6,000 breeding pairs. Declining. Upland specialist.
lakesPopulation: Breed on crags across the Lake District. Langdale and Borrowdale are reliable. The mountain blackbird.
description: The mountain blackbird. Ring ouzels breed on the crags. Langdale, Borrowdale, Helvellyn. That white breast band is the key. They're shy. You'll hear the chack call before you see them.
identification: Blackbird-sized. Black with white breast band. Female duller. Distinctive chack call.
tips: Walk the high crags in April and May. They're territorial then. Listen for the call. Keep your distance.

seasonalPresence: Year-round. Lekking display March to May.
bestTimeOfDay: Dawn. Lekking happens at first light.
whereToSee: Moorland edge and forestry. The Pennines and North Pennines hold the main English populations. Some in the eastern Lake District fells.
ukPopulation: Around 5,000 displaying males. Declining. Red list.
lakesPopulation: Small population in the eastern Lake District. Lekking sites are sensitive. Join a guided walk if you want to see them.
description: Black grouse lek at dawn. March to May. The eastern fells hold a small population. Lekking sites are protected. Don't go alone. Join a guided walk. RSPB and Wildlife Trusts run them.
identification: Male black with white wing bar and lyre-shaped tail. Female grey-brown, barred. Unmistakeable at a lek.
tips: Dawn only. Join a guided walk. Lekking sites are sensitive. Don't disturb.

seasonalPresence: Year-round. Most visible in winter when leaves are off the trees.
bestTimeOfDay: Morning. Active along streams throughout the day.
whereToSee: Fast-flowing streams and rivers. Stock Ghyll in Ambleside, the Rothay, the Brathay, any Lakeland beck. Look for them bobbing on rocks.
ukPopulation: Around 6,000 breeding pairs. Upland stream specialist.
lakesPopulation: Common on Lakeland becks. Stock Ghyll in Ambleside is one of the easiest. They're tame in the village.
diet: Aquatic invertebrates. Dives underwater to feed.
description: Dippers are the bird of Lakeland streams. Stock Ghyll in Ambleside is the easiest place. They bob on rocks, dive in, come up with insects. You can get close. Winter is best when the trees are bare.
identification: Plump, dark brown, white breast. Bobs constantly. Unmistakeable on a stream.
tips: Stock Ghyll in Ambleside. Walk up from the village. They're used to people. Winter gives the best views.

seasonalPresence: April to August. Summer visitor. Peak May and June.
bestTimeOfDay: Morning. Most active in the first few hours after dawn.
whereToSee: Oak woodland. RSPB Haweswater, Grizedale Forest, Borrowdale. Nest boxes have boosted populations.
ukPopulation: Around 35,000 breeding pairs. Declining. Nest box schemes help.
lakesPopulation: RSPB Haweswater and Grizedale are strongholds. Nest box trails make them easy to find. May is the best month.
diet: Flying insects. Catches them in the air.
description: Pied flycatchers love oak woodland. RSPB Haweswater has a nest box trail. Grizedale too. May is the month. The male is black and white, the female browner. You'll hear the song before you see them.
identification: Male black and white. Female brown with white wing patch. Small, active. Flicks tail.
tips: Follow the nest box trails at Haweswater and Grizedale. May and June. Early morning.

whereToSee: Oak and mixed woodland. RSPB Haweswater, Borrowdale, Grizedale. Often in the same woods as pied flycatchers.
lakesPopulation: Common in Lakeland oak woods. Borrowdale and Haweswater are reliable. That red tail is the giveaway.
diet: Insects. Catches them in flight and from leaves.
description: Redstarts share the oak woods with pied flycatchers. That red tail flicks as they hunt. Borrowdale, Haweswater, Grizedale. April to June. The song is a short warble. Once you know it you'll find them.
identification: Male grey back, black face, orange-red breast and tail. Female browner. Tail flicks constantly.
tips: Oak woodland in May. Listen for the song. They like mature trees with holes for nesting.

seasonalPresence: April to August. Summer visitor. Peak May and June.
whereToSee: Oak and beech woodland. Borrowdale, RSPB Haweswater, Grizedale. The spinning coin song is unmistakable.
ukPopulation: Around 6,000 breeding pairs. Declining. Amber list.
lakesPopulation: Borrowdale and Haweswater hold good numbers. Oak woodland. That song carries. Spinning coin then a descending trill.
description: Wood warblers have the best song in the Lakes. That spinning coin, then the trill. Borrowdale, Haweswater. Oak woodland. May. They're high in the canopy. Listen first.
identification: Yellow breast, green back, yellow supercilium. Larger than chiffchaff. That song is the key.
tips: May and June. Oak woodland. Listen for the spinning coin song. They're hard to see in the canopy.

seasonalPresence: Year-round. Irruptive. Numbers vary with cone crop.
bestTimeOfDay: Morning. Often heard before seen in conifer tops.
whereToSee: Conifer woodland. Grizedale Forest, Whinlatter, the forestry around Thirlmere. Listen for the chip-chip call.
ukPopulation: Around 40,000 breeding pairs. Irruptive. Good cone years bring more birds.
lakesPopulation: Grizedale and Whinlatter are the best bets. Conifer woodland. That crossed bill is for extracting seeds from cones.
diet: Conifer seeds. Extracted with the crossed bill.
description: Crossbills live in the conifers. Grizedale, Whinlatter. That chip-chip call from the treetops. The crossed bill is for cone seeds. Good cone years mean more birds. They're hard to see. Listen first.
identification: Finch with crossed bill. Male red, female green. Call is distinctive. Chip-chip-chip.
tips: Conifer woodland. Listen for the call. They're high in the trees. Grizedale and Whinlatter.

seasonalPresence: October to March. Winter visitor. Some stay into April.
bestTimeOfDay: Morning. Often in flocks on open water.
whereToSee: Ullswater, Windermere, Coniston. The larger lakes hold winter flocks. Diving ducks. Watch for the head-throw display.
ukPopulation: Around 27,000 wintering birds. Breed in Scotland and Scandinavia.
lakesPopulation: Ullswater and Windermere hold good numbers in winter. Coniston too. They dive for food. The male has a white face patch.
diet: Aquatic invertebrates, small fish. Dives to feed.
description: Goldeneyes winter on the lakes. Ullswater, Windermere, Coniston. Diving ducks. That head-throw display is something. The male has a white face patch. They're common. Easy to find.
identification: Medium diving duck. Male black and white with round white face patch. Female grey with brown head. Yellow eye.
tips: Winter. The big lakes. Ullswater is reliable. They dive. Watch for a few seconds and they'll go under.

bestTimeOfDay: Morning. Flushes from heather as you walk.
whereToSee: Heather moorland throughout the Lakes. The fells, Grizedale, Foulshaw Moss. That explosive flush. You'll hear them before you see them.
ukPopulation: Around 230,000 pairs. Moorland managed for grouse shooting.
lakesPopulation: Common on heather moorland across the Lakes. Walk any fell and you'll flush them.
description: Red grouse are the sound of the moorland. That explosive burst from the heather. Brown, barred. You'll flush them on most fell walks. Grizedale, Foulshaw Moss, the high fells. They're resident. No migration.
identification: Plump. Red-brown. Barred plumage. Short tail. Unmistakeable on moorland.
tips: Walk the heather. They'll flush. Don't chase. They're ground-nesting. Stick to paths in spring.

seasonalPresence: Year-round. Some winter visitors from the north.
whereToSee: Heather moorland. The fells, Foulshaw Moss, high ground. Small. Fast. Low flight.
lakesPopulation: Breed on moorland across the Lakes. Foulshaw Moss. The high fells. They're small. Don't confuse with kestrel. Merlin is darker, faster.
diet: Small birds. Meadow pipits, skylarks. Catches them in flight.
description: Merlins are the small falcon of the moorland. Foulshaw Moss. The fells. They hunt low and fast. Meadow pipits are the main prey. Spring and summer. Look for that dark, compact shape.
identification: Small falcon. Dark. Male blue-grey. Female brown. Fast, low flight. Smaller than kestrel.
tips: Moorland. Spring and summer. They're fast. Scan the sky. Don't confuse with kestrel. Merlin is darker, more compact.

seasonalPresence: March to October. Summer visitor. Passage birds in spring and autumn.
bestTimeOfDay: Morning. Perches on rocks and walls.
whereToSee: Upland grassland and crags. Langdale, Borrowdale, the Helvellyn range. That white rump as it flies.
lakesPopulation: Common on the fells. Langdale, Borrowdale. That white rump. They perch on walls and rocks. April to August.
diet: Insects. Catches them on the ground.
description: Wheatears are the bird of upland walls. That white rump as they fly. Langdale, Borrowdale. They perch on rocks. April to August. Easy to see. They're confiding.
identification: Robin-sized. Grey back. Black face mask. White rump. Orange breast. Unmistakeable in flight.
tips: Walk the fells. They like walls and rocks. The white rump is the giveaway. April to August.

seasonalPresence: April to September. Summer visitor. Passage in May and August.
whereToSee: The highest Lake District fells. Helvellyn, Scafell Pike, Great Gable. Above 700m. Rare. Don't disturb.
ukPopulation: Around 400 breeding pairs. Red list. Declining.
lakesPopulation: A few pairs on the highest fells. Helvellyn, Scafell. Passage birds in May. Stick to paths. They're ground-nesting.
description: Dotterels are rare. The highest fells only. Helvellyn, Scafell. That chestnut belly. The female is brighter than the male. Unusual. Stick to paths. Don't disturb nesting birds.
identification: Small plover. Grey breast band. Chestnut belly. White eyebrow. Female brighter than male.
tips: High fells only. May and June. Stick to paths. They're rare. Don't approach. Join a guided walk if you want to see them.

seasonalPresence: Year-round. Some winter visitors. Hunts by day.
bestTimeOfDay: Dusk and dawn. Also hunts in daylight.
whereToSee: Moorland and rough grassland. Foulshaw Moss, the fells, Martindale. That buoyant, quartering flight.
ukPopulation: Around 2,000 pairs. Fluctuates with vole numbers.
lakesPopulation: Foulshaw Moss. Martindale. Moorland. They hunt by day. That moth-like flight. Vole years mean more owls.
diet: Voles, small mammals. Hunts by day.
description: Short-eared owls hunt by day. Foulshaw Moss. Martindale. That buoyant, quartering flight. Vole years bring more. Dusk and dawn are best. They're nomadic. Numbers vary.
identification: Medium owl. Pale. Yellow eyes. Hunts by day. Buoyant flight. Long wings. The ears are barely visible.
tips: Moorland. Dusk and dawn. Vole years. Foulshaw Moss. Scan for that quartering flight. Don't confuse with harrier. Owl has rounder head.

seasonalPresence: Year-round. Some winter visitors. Breeding rare and persecuted.
bestTimeOfDay: Dusk and dawn. That low, quartering flight.
whereToSee: Moorland. Foulshaw Moss, the eastern fells. Rare. Persecuted. Winter is best. Birds come in from the uplands.
ukPopulation: Around 600 breeding pairs. Red list. Persecuted.
lakesPopulation: Foulshaw Moss in winter. Moorland. Rare. The grey male, brown female. Sky-dancing in spring. Don't expect to see one. Be pleased if you do.
diet: Small birds, mammals. Hunts by quartering.
description: Hen harriers are rare. Persecuted. Foulshaw Moss gets winter birds. That low, quartering flight. Grey male. Brown female. Sky-dancing in spring. Don't expect to see one. Report any persecution.
identification: Male grey with black wing tips. Female brown, barred. Long tail. Low, quartering flight. Owl-like face.
tips: Foulshaw Moss. Winter. Dusk and dawn. They're rare. Don't disclose nest sites. Persecution is still a problem.

whereToSee: Everywhere. Roadsides, grassland, moorland edge. That hover. The most visible falcon.
lakesPopulation: Common. Roadsides. Grassland. Moorland edge. You'll see them hovering. The valley roads. Borrowdale. Langdale.
diet: Voles, small mammals. Hovers to hunt.
description: Kestrels hover. That's the giveaway. Roadsides. Grassland. Borrowdale. Langdale. You'll see them from the car. They're declining. Used to be everywhere. Still common in the Lakes.
identification: Small falcon. Hovers. Male has grey head. Female brown. Long tail. Pointed wings.
tips: Roadsides. Look for the hover. They're common. Don't confuse with merlin. Kestrel hovers. Merlin doesn't.

whereToSee: Crags and fells. Honister, Langdale, Borrowdale. That cronk call. Bigger than crows.
lakesPopulation: Common on the fells. Honister. Langdale. Borrowdale. That cronk. They soar. They're big. Unmistakeable.
description: Ravens are the sound of the crags. That cronk. Honister. Langdale. They soar. Big. Much bigger than crows. They've recovered. Used to be persecuted. Now they're everywhere on the fells.
identification: Large. All black. Wedge-shaped tail. Deep cronk call. Bigger than carrion crow.
tips: The fells. Honister. Listen for the cronk. They soar. Unmistakeable.

seasonalPresence: Year-round. Sings from March to July.
bestTimeOfDay: Morning. That song flight. Straight up, then hover.
whereToSee: Moorland and grassland. The fells, Foulshaw Moss, rough pasture. That song. Unmistakeable.
ukPopulation: Around 1.5 million territories. Declining. Red list.
lakesPopulation: Common on moorland and rough grassland. The fells. That song flight. March to July. You'll hear them before you see them.
description: Skylarks sing in the air. That ascending song. Moorland. The fells. March to July. They're declining. Red list. But still common in the Lakes. That song is the sound of spring.
identification: Brown. Streaked. Crest. Sings in flight. Straight up, then hover. Unmistakeable song.
tips: Moorland. Spring. Listen for the song. They're hard to see on the ground. The song flight gives them away.

whereToSee: Everywhere on moorland and grassland. The fells. Foulshaw Moss. That parachute display. The most common pipit.
lakesPopulation: Abundant. Moorland. Grassland. You'll flush them on every walk. That pip-pip call. They're the food of merlins.
description: Meadow pipits are everywhere on the moorland. You'll flush them. That pip-pip. They do a parachute display in spring. Straight up, then glide down. Merlins eat them. The most common bird on the fells.
identification: Brown. Streaked. Pipit. Walks. Flushes. Parachute display. Similar to tree pipit. Meadow is more common on open ground.
tips: Walk the moorland. You'll see them. They're common. The parachute display in spring is worth watching.

seasonalPresence: Year-round. Breeding on moorland. Winter on coast.
whereToSee: Moorland and rough grassland. The fells, Foulshaw Moss, Martindale. That call. Unmistakeable.
ukPopulation: Around 58,000 breeding pairs. Red list. Declining fast.
lakesPopulation: Breed on moorland across the Lakes. That bubbling call. Martindale. Foulshaw Moss. They're declining. Worth seeking out. The call is the sound of the uplands.
diet: Worms, insects. Long bill for probing.
description: Curlews have the best call. That bubbling. Moorland. Martindale. Foulshaw Moss. They're declining. Red list. The long curved bill. They probe for worms. Spring and summer. Listen for the call.
identification: Large wader. Long curved bill. Brown. That bubbling call. Unmistakeable.
tips: Moorland. Spring. Listen for the call. They're ground-nesting. Stick to paths. They're declining. Enjoy them while they're here.

seasonalPresence: Year-round. Breeding on wet ground. Winter visitors too.
whereToSee: Wet grassland and bog. Foulshaw Moss, wet flushes, the fells. That drumming. Zigzag flight when flushed.
lakesPopulation: Foulshaw Moss. Wet ground. That drumming in spring. The outer tail feathers vibrate. They zigzag when flushed. Wet flushes on the fells.
diet: Worms, insects. Long bill for probing.
description: Snipes drum. That weird vibrating sound in spring. Foulshaw Moss. Wet ground. The outer tail feathers do it. They zigzag when flushed. Long bill. Striped head. Wet flushes on the fells.
identification: Long bill. Striped head. Brown. Zigzag flight. Drumming in spring. Unmistakeable.
tips: Wet ground. Spring for the drumming. Dusk. Foulshaw Moss. They're cryptic. You'll hear them before you see them.

whereToSee: Lakeshores and riverbanks. Ullswater, Windermere, the Rothay. That bobbing. The teeter. Unmistakeable.
lakesPopulation: Common on Lakeland shores. Ullswater. Windermere. That bobbing teeter. They fly low over the water. April to August.
description: Common sandpipers bob. That teeter. Lakeshores. Ullswater. Windermere. They fly low over the water. Stiff wings. April to August. Easy to see. They're confiding.
identification: Brown. White breast. Bobs. Teeters. Flies low with stiff wings. Unmistakeable.
tips: Lakeshores. April to August. They bob. That teeter. Easy to see. Ullswater is reliable.

seasonalPresence: Year-round. Some winter visitors. Breeding by fast water.
whereToSee: Fast streams and rivers. Stock Ghyll, the Rothay, any Lakeland beck. That yellow underparts. Long tail.
lakesPopulation: Common on Lakeland streams. Stock Ghyll. The Rothay. That yellow. They wag their tail. Fast water. Don't confuse with yellow wagtail. Grey has grey back.
description: Grey wagtails love fast water. Stock Ghyll. The Rothay. That yellow underparts. They wag. Long tail. Don't confuse with yellow wagtail. Grey has a grey back. Year-round on the becks.
identification: Grey back. Yellow underparts. Long tail. Wags. Fast water. Male has black throat in summer.
tips: Fast streams. Stock Ghyll. They're common. That yellow. Don't confuse with yellow wagtail.

whereToSee: Woodland edge. Grizedale, Whinlatter, Borrowdale. That parachute song flight. Drops from a tree.
lakesPopulation: Grizedale. Whinlatter. Woodland edge. That song flight. Drops from a tree, then parachutes down. April to June. Declining.
description: Tree pipits sing from trees. Grizedale. Whinlatter. That parachute. They drop, then glide. Different to meadow pipit. Tree has a stronger bill. Woodland edge. April to June. Declining.
identification: Pipit. Streaked. Song flight from tree. Parachutes down. Stronger bill than meadow. Woodland edge.
tips: Woodland edge. April to June. Listen for the song. The parachute flight. Grizedale. Whinlatter.

bestTimeOfDay: Morning. Drumming in March and April.
whereToSee: Woodland throughout the Lakes. Grizedale, Borrowdale, Whinlatter. That drum. The red patch.
lakesPopulation: Common in Lakeland woodland. Grizedale. Borrowdale. That drum in spring. Red undertail. Male has red nape. Easy to hear.
description: Great spotted woodpeckers drum. That rapid tap. Grizedale. Borrowdale. March and April. Red undertail. Male has red nape. They're common. You'll hear them before you see them.
identification: Black and white. Red undertail. Male has red nape. Drumming. Unmistakeable.
tips: Woodland. Spring. Listen for the drum. Grizedale. Borrowdale. They're common.

whereToSee: Woodland throughout the Lakes. Grizedale, Borrowdale, RSPB Haweswater. Creeps up trunks. Spiral.
lakesPopulation: Common in Lakeland woodland. Grizedale. Borrowdale. They creep up trunks. Spiral. That curved bill. Small. Brown. Easy to miss.
description: Treecreepers creep. Up tree trunks. Spiral. Grizedale. Borrowdale. That curved bill. Small. Brown. They're common. Look for movement on the trunk. They never come down head first.
identification: Small. Brown. Streaked. Curved bill. Creeps up trunks. Spiral. Never head first down.
tips: Woodland. Look at tree trunks. They creep. Spiral up. Grizedale. Borrowdale. They're common.

whereToSee: Woodland. Grizedale, Borrowdale, the eastern Lakes. That blue-grey back. Climbs down head first.
lakesPopulation: Grizedale. Borrowdale. Oak woodland. That blue-grey. They climb down head first. Unlike treecreeper. That pew-pew call. Expanding in the Lakes.
diet: Insects, nuts. Wedges nuts in bark.
description: Nuthatches climb down head first. Treecreepers don't. That blue-grey back. Grizedale. Borrowdale. That pew-pew call. They wedge nuts in bark and hack them open. Expanding north. More in the Lakes now.
identification: Blue-grey back. Orange underparts. Climbs down head first. Strong bill. Unmistakeable.
tips: Oak woodland. Grizedale. Borrowdale. That pew-pew. They climb down. Treecreepers don't.

whereToSee: Woodland. Grizedale, Borrowdale, RSPB Haweswater. Hard to separate from willow tit. Call is the key.
lakesPopulation: Grizedale. Borrowdale. Oak woodland. That pitchoo call. Marsh tit. Willow tit is similar. Marsh has glossy cap. Call is the best way.
description: Marsh tits are hard to separate from willow tits. The call helps. Pitchoo. Marsh has glossy cap. Willow has dull cap. Grizedale. Borrowdale. Both declining. Red list.
identification: Small tit. Black cap. Glossy. Pitchoo call. Similar to willow tit. Cap and call are the keys.
tips: Learn the call. Pitchoo. Marsh. Woodland. Grizedale. Borrowdale. They're declining.

whereToSee: Wet woodland and scrub. Foulshaw Moss, Grizedale, damp areas. That chay-chay-chay call. Declining fast.
ukPopulation: Around 3,500 pairs. Red list. Declining fast.
lakesPopulation: Foulshaw Moss. Wet woodland. That chay call. Dull cap. Different to marsh tit. Declining. Worth looking for. Wet scrub.
description: Willow tits are declining fast. Foulshaw Moss. Wet woodland. That chay-chay-chay. Dull cap. Marsh tit has glossy. The call is different. They excavate nest holes. Wet scrub. Red list.
identification: Small tit. Dull black cap. Chay call. Similar to marsh. Dull cap. Wet habitat.
tips: Wet woodland. Foulshaw Moss. Learn the call. Chay. They're rare. Declining. Worth the search.

seasonalPresence: Year-round. More in winter at feeders.
whereToSee: Conifer woodland. Grizedale, Whinlatter, Borrowdale. That tzooee call. Yellow and black. Small finch.
lakesPopulation: Grizedale. Whinlatter. Conifer woodland. That tzooee. Yellow and black. They flock. Winter at feeders. Common.
description: Siskins are small finches. Yellow and black. That tzooee call. Grizedale. Whinlatter. Conifer tops. They flock. Winter at feeders. Common in the Lakes.
identification: Small finch. Yellow and black. Streaked. Tzooee call. Conifer woodland.
tips: Conifer woodland. Grizedale. Whinlatter. Listen for the call. Winter at feeders.
